sentences.text: It was-- it was very scary experience, because they gave us a short time to move into the ghetto.
And we lived in one room.
Five people lived in one room.
It was very crowded.
The conditions were very unhealthy.
And the nutrition was very poor.
But somehow, we felt a little relaxation.
We felt-- you know, there were stores.
They had a hospital.
They had a old age home, a orphanage home.
And my-- I remember my mother selling some of her jewelry to get some food.
Because really, we-- the nutrition was very poor.
And I remember selling the silver candlesticks and some of her jewelery.
And we felt like maybe that the terror that we were previously exposed to had passed.
We really didn't expect that so soon it will begin again.
And it did.
Like, in June-- you know, in June it started all over again.
They-- they started to talk about-- rumors started to circulate.
And I had overheard my parents saying that they talking about the German to diminish the size of the ghetto.
Yes, I heard.
My father came.
He said, you know-- he just walked in, in a room.
He says, you know, everybody's talking that the German are going diminish the size of the ghetto.
And-- and then-- and then I heard my mother answering, well, that means they will be probably sending people away, deportation.
And this is what actually happened.
I remember in June we saw-- they told us later, this is a special Gestapo that circles around the ghetto.
And very soon they started to ]PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just grab people from the street-- old people, children.
And-- and-- and they were put on the trucks and shipped away.
You know, they loaded them on the trucks.
And all of the sudden, they disappeared.
And this is when the terror begun again.
My father was working.
My father really had a-- in Krakow, he had a shop and employed some people.
And he did what we would call here-- he was a contractor for iron work, for buildings.
And I remember him telling us that the Gestapo had taken him to do this kind of work.
He spoke fluently, fluently German, my dad.
Most of his equipment, machineries, came from Vienna.
And he was able to do this kind of work for them.
And we thought he was safe.
But unfortunately, he was not.
Because the deportation went on daily.
And they-- the Germans were so shrewd, I would call, knowing that people probably hide in attics or-- or cellars.
So they came in, breaking the doors, and dragging people out from their hidings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And that's when my older sister tried-- she was going out to work, outside the ghetto.
And she thought that maybe if she will take my mother out to have safety, because they started to take people like, over 40.
That's considered old, right?
So she thought maybe my mother would-- would be able to be saved.
And she tried to get a old friend of ours that we used to live as a-- when I was a young child, you know?
And she went to one of those neighbors and ask her if she could possibly keep my mother overnight.
And she actually slammed the door on her.
And she says, get out.
You a Jew.
And this was a very poor experience, you know, a very-- very disappointing experience for my sister, because those were my mother's friends.
But I guess they were scared, too.
But somehow, she managed to find someone to hold her over that night.
And that was the night when they came in.
Two SS men walked into our room, armed, and dragged my father out.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] He-- he turned around to us, tried to put us at ease, and he says, don't worry, children.
They taking me to work.
They need me.
I mean, he was so sure.
He's working for the Gestapo.
He speaks fluently German.
He has such a wonderful trade.
[SIGHS] But we-- we all really hoped and believed that they are taken to work and being resettled.
We were completely unaware of what was going on.
Nobody even assumed that [SIGHS] they were taken away to their death.
I was just looking, staring at him.
And he just, with his sweet smile, turned and he said, don't you worry, don't you worry.
I'll be OK.
They need me.
Those were his words, his last words.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]
They assembled [SOBS] the people on the square.
It was a square, where they kept the people over sometimes, day and night, without food, without water, in the September heat, where people were just sitting there.
But then seeing how they were dragging people, and kicking, and pushing.
People who couldn't walk fast enough-- they kicked them.
They shot them on the spot.
There were corpses lying in the streets, blood all over.
And there was a little bit doubt in our mind what really-- what is happening?
Still, no one, no one-- we were completely ignorant about the extermination camps.
Nobody even thought that anything like this exists.
Well, I later found out that my-- my dear father perished in Belzec.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some people that were with him in cattle car.
And I think, from what my friends told me who escaped-- and they ask my father to follow, to come with him.

But I think my father-- and it's unbelievable, because my father was always such a happy individual and-- but at that point, from what my friends are telling me, he must have given up, because he sat very quietly and refused to jump.
But thinking about it back, my dad had a condition.
He-- he was supposed to be operated.
He had a hernia.
And when the SS men came in and grabbed him, he didn't even take his truss that he was wearing to support his condition, hernia condition.
So it's dreadful [SIGHS] for me to tell you this, but thinking about this, how he must have suffered those few days with this condition, that probably that's why he didn't jump out the window.
But those two people did jump out.
And they told me that my father was sitting, like, giving up.
I mean, he was a very smart man.
At that point, he already must have known that it's not very good anymore.

It wasn't the same man that I remembered, from what my friends told me how he sat-- was sitting there and didn't even try to jump, while those two friends of mine did jump out the-- through the window, from the running train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I did meet a gentleman in our society, Krakow society, that always was telling me and my sister how my dear father was always trying to help him financially.
He-- he had nine children.
And he says, every Friday your father would come to me and put some money in my pocket.
He said, here, you probably can use it.
Those are the things that I hear about my parents.
My mother used to go to a psychiatric hospital twice a week.
I was a little girl.
And she used to cook some food with some of her neighbors and carry the food.
And I remember holding on to her, and she would go to a psychiatric hospital and feed people, where there were shortage of nurses.
I remember sitting with my little doll in the waiting room and watching her feeding the people.
They were shaking.
They were moving constantly.
And this is the kind of-- of parents I had.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I don't think they deserved this kind of death.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Well--
I was with my sisters.
And I think if my mother would be around, probably she would be taken away, too.
So-- [SOBBING] but since that day, my mother was not the same.
When she-- when my sister brought her back the next day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything changed for her.
I just remember-- I recall that every time somebody knocked on the door, I always saw her face with anticipation.
And she probably was hoping that he's coming back.
Because I saw her face, always staring at the door, like-- and then like a let down.
Those two people were so much in love.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
I think that her life stopped suddenly, you know?
I mean, she had us.
And I know that she tried very hard.
But it wasn't the same woman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her sense of humor sort of diminished.
And we kept on, because the life in the ghetto was very, very scary.
It was-- they pushed us together.
The size of the ghetto became smaller and smaller.
They-- we received labor cards.
And each time we went to work, we-- we had another initial added to it-- W, R-- I don't even recall the initials which meant something.
And they divided the ghetto in ghetto A and B.
And all the people that lost their jobs had to be in the ghetto B, which meant deportation.
The people that worked were in the ghetto A.
And some of those people were already being sent to build barracks in-- in a camp that was built nearby, Camp Plaszow.
I was still in ghetto A, until the day I lost my job.
And they were giving out additional blue cards to the-- to the labor cards.
And I didn't receive my blue card anymore.
That was-- because the ghetto is getting smaller.
And there was less work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at that time, Amon Goeth was sent to liquidate the ghetto.
Because slowly, people were being sent to Camp Plaszow.
No.
No, I just knew that one of the Lublin ghetto was liquidated.
And Amon Goeth, after liquidation of the Lublin ghetto, came to Krakow, to liquidate the ghetto in Krakow.
Where do--
No.
I saw-- but the-- the-- [SIGHS] the hardest time was also to see what they did with the nursing home.
They murdered all the people.
They took most of the children from the orphanage home on the trucks, sent them away, and the rest were murdered.
They ordered all the doctors and nurses from the hospital to come out.
Whoever wouldn't come out, they shot him on the spot.
And they murdered all the people in the hospital.
It was a real massacre.
This was-- it was like blood all over the ghetto, whatever was left of it.
I was-- I was already in my little room, in our room, because at the time I didn't work.
But then suddenly we heard that everybody has to report to the consul with their-- show their identification, their labor cards.
I was standing on the line to report to the consul.
And right in front of the line, I saw my sister's friend, Basia.
And I saw her in her blue, loose coat.
And she reached into her pocket and she took out a a bunch of money.
And she was giving it to the SS man.
I assume she wanted him to let her go to the good side.
There was at bad side, to the deportation.
Evidently, she didn't have her blue card.
And suddenly, I saw how he grabbed the money from her, and with the rifle, he pushed her sideways.
We never saw her again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that moment, simultaneously-- you know, it's amazing how your mind works, how without planning, I looked to my left.
And I saw a man loading large cans of milk.
You know, the supply of food was being sent from the ghetto to the camp.
So the man was-- was putting large cans of milk on a wagon, with a horse.
And they were covered with a green blanket.
And as he walked away to get some more supply, I-- I hopped on-- on the wagon, next to those cans of milk, and I covered myself this blanket.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was my luck, because I would have never made it.
I didn't have my blue card.
And this is how I got into Camp Plaszow.
As I said before, I saw Basia being pushed to the other side.
I knew I don't have my blue card, which meant I was unemployed.
I was-- I belonged to the camp B.
So at that moment, when I looked and I saw him loading the milk, I said, you know, the blanket is there.
He won't see me.
He will think I'm next to the cans of milk.
As it was, he saw me, because unfortunately, he wanted revenge.
I-- it was a very bad experience when I got to Camp Plaszow, because it was almost getting dark.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he-- when I came-- when I got off, he grabbed me to the fence.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he tried to rape me.
Well, [SIGHS] I don't know where my strength came, but I kicked him so hard, below his stomach, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I started to run so fast.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My back was scratched from the wires.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, the camp was surrounded with barbed wires.
He pushed me toward them, you know.
And I was running so fast.
My sister and my mother was already there, you know, in the camp.
Betty followed.
Betty was with me in the ghetto still.
My sister Betty was still working.
She came in with the rest of the people that arrived to Plaszow.
See, the people that were standing on the line-- the one that had all-- all the proper stamps and blue card went to Camp Plaszow, OK?
And the rest were deported.
We never saw those people again.
Most of the people are not around anymore.
And when I came-- Camp Plaszow, you know, Susan, was built on two Jewish cemeteries, as you know.
And it was surrounded with double barbed wires.
And in between was like a ditch filled with water.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The German called it a labor camp.
But to-- you know, in reality, it was a extermination camp.
Because there was daily hanging and killing, mutilations, 25 lashes.
So it wasn't just a work camp.
It was an extermination camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From the beginning, the prisoners-- they called us the prisoners, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They ordered to desecrate the tombstones of our [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] grandfathers.
And they built--
Yeah, of course.
Of course.
And they built-- from those tombstones, they ordered them to build roads and walks in the camp.
Sometimes you could see names walking on-- excuse me.
[DRINKS] The first few days in the camp, I was ordered to clean barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the third day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Amon Goeth walked into the barrack for inspection.
He looked around, very similar to the movie where he walks down where the girls are standing in a row.
That's exactly how it happened in that barrack.
He walked around, looked at every girl.
Suddenly, he stopped in front of me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said to the woman that was in charge of us-- he says, if a Jewish girl is smart enough to clean a window on a sunny day, she probably is good for me.
Send her to my house.
I wasn't aware what I am getting into, believe me.
I wasn't aware.
He was a huge individual.
He used to bend his head walking through the door.
He was a very tall, husky man.
And the only way I can describe him after being under his roof for almost two years-- I can't even call him a man.
I think he was a wild animal in a human body.
Because animals could be nice, but he was a wild animal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next day--
I just thought I'm going to be working for another German, like I used to in the ghetto.
I didn't know who he was.
But I found out that he's the Kommandant of the Camp Plaszow.
He is the only one who gives all the orders in the camp, who is the only one-- he's the only one to decide who is to live and who is to die.
But I still didn't think that working for him he could be as cruel as he really was.
I arrived in this house that was up on the hill, near the barracks.
It was a small house.
When I came in, there was another woman there.
And he asked me my name.
And I said, I'm Helena Sternlicht.
He says, well, I have one Helena.
You going to be Susanna.
And Helena will be, from now on, Lena.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
She was there, supposedly, already for a couple of weeks.
And I was supposed to be the second servant.
My very first experience with him was-- was when I was taught to iron his shirt.
Did I know how to iron a shirt?
No.
I never did anything home.
My mom never let us do anything.
I was the baby.
All of a sudden, I felt a pull from the back, by my braids, and a slap on my cheek that everything swirled in front of me.
And I started to cry.
And he hit me once more, even harder.
He says, in my house, I don't want see any sad faces.
Do you understand?
And from a distance, I saw my friend shaking her head.
You know?
Your-- the other--
Helen, yeah, the other Helen.
She was-- she saw-- I guess she knew him already for those few weeks.
She was older.
She was, I think, 12, 14 years older than me.
She spoke fluently German.
I had to learn the hard way.
She helped me a lot.
She taught me a lot, because she knew how to iron a shirt by then.
And I learned so fast.
I grew up so fast, because I had to.
Because when he said to me, in my house, I don't want to see sad faces, and in Austria, a girl your age knows how to iron a shirt properly, but you too stupid.
You are a Jew.
I had to learn.
Because if I didn't learn, I was getting so much beating.
Under the slightest provocation, I received the most cruel punishment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know that I remember my ears most of the time ringing.
I always had the ringing in my ears.
Because he would slap me on the slightest provocation.
If I wouldn't look straight in his face, if I-- if he talked to me and I would look sideways, there it was.
And how many times I rolled down those stairs in the villa later, that I am surprised that I only have this condition in my neck that I found out later.
The doctors asked me if I had a whiplash maybe, in a car accident.
I never had this kind of car accident.
But then I realized how many times my head was swinging to the left and right.
And that's why I had this bad condition in my neck.
But before the villa, I was another house.
Oh yeah, we-- we later moved down to another house.
It was called the red brick house.
This was one house, up next to the barracks.
It was a small house.
But then, shortly after, we moved down to a big house, a brick house.
This was right near the entrance of the camp.
And across this brick house, red brick house, were the offices, where-- where he had his office, and some of the other officers were there.
And it was the first aid there for the Germans.
In that red brick house, we started to have to exchange our positions at night.
During the day, we were together.
At night, one of us had to go to sleep, to the barrack.
And one remained on duty.
Because he started to have guests.
He started to entertain.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- [SIGHS] I experienced this terrible night that I was on duty-- and--
OK.
